questions.text: Federalism is a system of government in which power is divided between a central authority and:
options: Various constituent units of the country
questions.explanation: Usually, a federation has two levels of government: one for the entire country and others at the level of provinces or states.

questions.text: In a unitary system of government:
options: There is only one level of government or sub-units are subordinate to the central government
questions.explanation: The central government can pass on orders to the provincial or local governments in a unitary system.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a key feature of federalism?
options: Two or more levels of government
Different tiers govern the same citizens
The center can change the constitution unilaterally
Sources of revenue are clearly specified
questions.explanation: Fundamental provisions of the constitution cannot be unilaterally changed by one level of government in a federation.

questions.text: Which type of federation involves independent states coming together on their own to form a bigger unit?
questions.explanation: Examples include the USA, Switzerland and Australia.

questions.text: India, Spain and Belgium are examples of which type of federation?
questions.explanation: In this system, a large country decides to divide its power between the constituent states and the national government.

questions.text: Which list includes subjects of national importance such as defense, foreign affairs, banking, and currency?
questions.explanation: Only the Union Government can make laws on the subjects mentioned in the Union List.

questions.text: Which list contains subjects of common interest to both the Union and State Governments, such as education, forest, trade unions, marriage, and adoption?
questions.explanation: Both governments can make laws, but if their laws conflict, the law made by the Union Government prevails.

questions.text: Subjects like computer software that came up after the constitution was made are called:
questions.explanation: According to our constitution, the Union Government has the power to legislate on these 'residuary' subjects.

questions.text: Which of the following Indian states had its own constitution until 2019 (under Article 370)?
questions.explanation: Article 370 gave special status to J&K, which was revoked in August 2019.

questions.text: Areas which are too small to become an independent state but which could not be merged with any of the existing states are called:
questions.explanation: Examples include Chandigarh, Lakshadweep and the capital city of Delhi.

questions.text: The creation of ________ was the first and a major test for democratic politics in India.
questions.explanation: After 1947, boundaries of several old states were changed to create new states to ensure people who spoke the same language lived in the same state.

questions.text: How many languages are recognized as Scheduled Languages by the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: These languages are listed in the Eighth Schedule of the Constitution.

questions.text: According to the Constitution, the use of English for official purposes was to stop in:
questions.explanation: However, many non-Hindi speaking states demanded that the use of English continue.

questions.text: When no single party gets a clear majority in the Lok Sabha, the major national parties form an alliance with many regional parties to form a:
questions.explanation: This led to a new culture of power sharing and respect for the autonomy of State Governments.

questions.text: When power is taken away from Central and State governments and given to local government, it is called:
questions.explanation: The basic idea is that there are many problems which are best settled at the local level.

questions.text: A major step towards decentralization was taken in ________ by amending the constitution.
questions.explanation: The 73rd and 74th Amendments made the third tier of democracy more powerful and effective.

questions.text: What percentage of seats are reserved for women in local government bodies in India?
questions.explanation: At least one-third of all positions are reserved for women.

questions.text: The head of the Gram Panchayat is called:
questions.explanation: The Sarpanch is the president of the village council.

questions.text: A few Gram Panchayats are grouped together to form what is usually called a:
questions.explanation: It is also known as a block or mandal.

questions.text: All the Panchayat Samitis or Mandals in a district together constitute the:
questions.explanation: The Zila Parishad chairperson is the political head of the district level local government.

questions.text: The political head of a Municipal Corporation is the:
questions.explanation: Municipal Corporations are the local government bodies for big cities.

questions.text: Which body conducts elections for Panchayats and Municipalities in each state?
questions.explanation: An independent institution called the State Election Commission has been created in each state.

questions.text: The system of 'Panchayati Raj' is an example of:
questions.explanation: At the village level, through the Gram Sabha, citizens participate directly in decision making.

questions.text: Which of the following is NOT a level of government in the Indian federation?
questions.explanation: India has a three-tier system: Union, State, and Local (Panchayats/Municipalities).

questions.text: In a holding together federation, the central government tends to be ________ than the states.
questions.explanation: Very often different constituent units of the federation have unequal powers.

questions.text: What is the role of the Judiciary in a federal system?
options: To interpret the constitution and settle disputes between levels of government
questions.explanation: Courts act as an umpire if disputes arise between different levels of government.

questions.text: Which state was NOT created on the basis of language but to recognize differences based on culture, ethnicity or geography?
questions.explanation: States like Nagaland, Uttarakhand and Jharkhand were created for these reasons.

questions.text: The Hindi language is the mother tongue of only about ________ percent of Indians.
questions.explanation: Therefore, there were many safeguards to protect other languages.

questions.text: The period after 1990 saw the rise of ________ political parties in many states of the country.
questions.explanation: The rise of regional parties led to the era of coalition governments at the Centre.

questions.text: The 1992 amendment made it constitutionally mandatory to hold regular elections to local government bodies.
questions.explanation: Before 1992, elections were not held regularly and local bodies lacked power.

questions.text: The Gram Sabha consists of:
options: All the voters in the village
questions.explanation: It has to meet at least twice or thrice in a year to approve the annual budget.

questions.text: Which institution has been strengthened by the system of local government in India?
questions.explanation: Local government is the largest experiment in democracy conducted anywhere in the world.

questions.text: One of the dual objectives of a federal system is to safeguard and promote the unity of the country while at the same time accommodate:
questions.explanation: Accommodation of diversity is a core reason for adopting federalism.

questions.text: Which of these is a subject in the State List?
questions.explanation: Police, trade, commerce, agriculture and irrigation are in the State List.

questions.text: If there is a conflict in the Concurrent List between State and Union law, which law prevails?
questions.explanation: The Constitution specifies that the law made by the Union Government shall prevail.

questions.text: Which state has a special provision under Article 371 of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: Article 371 provides special provisions for several states to protect their unique cultures and interests.

questions.text: The vertical division of power is also known as:
questions.explanation: It involves sharing power among governments at different levels.

questions.text: Jurisdiction is the ________ over which someone has legal authority.
questions.explanation: The area may be defined in terms of geographical boundaries or in terms of certain kinds of subjects.

questions.text: Which tier of government was added to the Indian federation by the 1992 amendment?
questions.explanation: A third-tier of government in the form of Panchayats and Municipalities was added.

questions.text: The success of federalism in India is attributed to its:
questions.explanation: The respect for diversity and desire for living together became shared ideals in our country.
